title: AI Agents: Who's Really in Charge?

modules.title: Meet the Agent Running Your World
modules.sub: Discover what AI agents are and how they operate
modules.description: Learners step into the role of an oversight officer in a near-future city and encounter AI agents already making real decisions around them. They explore what makes an AI system an 'agent' — something that perceives its environment, makes decisions, and takes action — and begin questioning how much trust these systems deserve.

modules.title: Goals, Tools, and Decision Loops
modules.sub: Trace how agents sense, plan, and act on their goals
modules.description: Learners map out the perception-planning-action loop that drives agent behaviour, using city scenarios like traffic control and hospital bed allocation as live examples. They experiment with how changing an agent's goal or available tools shifts its decisions, building intuition for why design choices matter from the very start.

modules.title: When Agents Act Without Asking
modules.sub: Explore what happens when autonomy goes unchecked
modules.description: Learners face a series of high-stakes moments where agents take consequential actions — rejecting a job applicant, rerouting an ambulance — without checking with any human. They analyse the conditions that lead to autonomous overreach and debate where the line between helpful automation and dangerous independence should sit.

modules.title: Multi-Agent Chaos: Who Decides?
modules.sub: Untangle conflicts when multiple agents share the same space
modules.description: Learners observe what happens when several AI agents with different goals operate in the same environment and start working against each other. They trace cascading failures caused by agents optimising in isolation and must decide which system to override first, developing a framework for understanding coordination risks in multi-agent setups.

modules.title: Pulling the Plug: Control and Limits
modules.sub: Define when and how humans must step back in
modules.description: Learners examine the tools humans use to maintain oversight — kill switches, permission boundaries, audit logs, and fallback protocols — and stress-test each one against realistic failure scenarios. They weigh the trade-offs between efficiency and control, arriving at principles for designing systems that remain correctable even when things go wrong fast.

modules.title: Build Your Own Agent Brief
modules.sub: Design a safe, bounded agent for a real-world problem
modules.description: Learners apply everything from the course to produce a structured design brief for an AI agent of their choosing, specifying its goal, perception inputs, action boundaries, and mandatory human oversight rules. They peer-review each other's briefs against a shared safety checklist, practising the kind of critical evaluation that responsible AI deployment demands.

synopsis: Step inside a near-future city where AI agents manage traffic, hiring, and hospital beds — and you're the oversight officer deciding when to trust them and when to intervene. Through high-stakes choices and cascading consequences, you'll learn exactly how autonomous agents perceive, plan, and act, and why human judgment still matters.
rationale: As AI agents move from research labs into everyday infrastructure and personal devices, teenagers need a working mental model of how they operate and a critical framework for evaluating when autonomous action is appropriate — before they become the adults deploying or governed by these systems.
learning_outcome: Students can explain the perception-planning-action loop that drives AI agents, identify failure modes and control risks in multi-agent systems, and produce a structured design brief that defines goals, boundaries, and human oversight rules for a real-world agent scenario.
source_signals: Launch HN: AgentHub (YC W24) – A no-code automation platform
